title: 2025 11 19 - HE
description: 19 November 2025 | Health Committee Briefing on endometriosis Endometriosis New Zealand Tanya Cooke, Chief Executive Petition of Judy Anderson: Inquiry into advice on GLP-1s for obese children and possible dietary solutions Judy Anderson Glen Davies
descriptionSummary: Briefing on endometriosis. Endometriosis New Zealand. Tanya Cooke, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Any very you here, you, thank you for, for coming in front of the committee today. We have you here for 10 minutes. Uh, you may use that time as you wish. If you want to give us time for questions, you may, but I'll hand that to you now. Thank you, Koto. Thank you very much for providing me the opportunity to present to you today.

I have, um, supplied you with a briefing, um, paper, which I'll talk to briefly, and I'm happy to answer any questions at the end of the, um, session. Um, what, what I'd like to focus on now is, um, why we need to act now and what action needs to be, um, put in place.

First of all, the scale of the impact of endometriosis in New Zealand. Um, what we know that one in 10 women girls and there's a fact signed female of both are living, um, with the condition that is a conservative estimate currently overseas in Australia.

There's research that states one in seven, so that equates to a minimum of 120,000 people with a condition. Um, new studies, uh, are stating that the economic burden to New Zealand is in the billions. We're waiting for the publication of a endo cost study, which is a national burden of illness study that's been in the pipeline for about five years.

That's due to publication in the next few months. And it, the numbers are staggering, that it does, um, definitely states that the cost of the, um, illness is in the billions to the society in New Zealand. We are also currently, um, underway with a research project partnering with the University of Canterbury.

And within our study we have found that on average endometriosis patients, um, have to take eight hours a week off work. It affects their productivity. So that billions is the cost. It's not, um, the cost of the healthcare system, but it's actually productivity issue.

So, um, it really does need to be seen as, as a big issue. Um, not only does it impact people's, um, ability to be product productive at work, but it impacts education. Many young girls, um, go and attend school all the way throughout the month.

Many we hear constantly of young girls having to, um, drop out of university and also has a huge impact on relationships and quality of life. So what do we, you, you know, so, um, definitely I feel that needs to be recognized as a public health issue, but what, um, what can we do to change the outcomes of this considerable number of, um, people in New Zealand with the condition?

Well, there is an existing new guideline that's been developed in our Australia for Australia by Rands Cog, and that is ready to be adapted here in New Zealand. So, um, rands Cog have endorsed it. We're encouraging Health New Zealand to adapt those guidelines.

The guidelines, um, do, um, require an an investment and there's quite a lot of changes that will need to be incurred, but there will be more of a focus of a diagnosis through imaging rather than through an invasive surgery. If there is the right investment that's put into ultrasounds training of ultrasounds equipment, making sure that there's a a solid referral pathway, this will have a huge change on their, the trajectory for endometriosis patients.

We currently know in New Zealand, there's a 10 year diagnostic delay. Wow. Uh, yeah, that's because we're waiting for gynecologists to, um, to conduct a surgery to currently diagnose. The concept recognizes that obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, dementia, polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, these are all the same condition.

They have one cause and therefore they have one effective treatment. And 50% or 49% of general practice consultations are for metabolic health or with the same cause. The second word, ultra processed food, ultra processed food. These are foods that are manufactured. They come in packets.

They generally contain added sugar and seed oils. They are reconstituted from commodity products that are high in fructose corn syrup, flour, vegetable oils. Um, they've been manufactured to maximize their addictiveness. The industry calls it bliss point.
